Int. J. Electron. Commun. (AEÜ) 200 (2025) 155870  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect  
Int. J. Electron. Commun. (AEÜ)  
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/aeue  
TimeBoost: Accelerating timing analysis engines using XGBoost  
∗
Anastasis Vagenas  
, Dimitrios Garyfallou , Nestor Evmorfopoulos , George Stamoulis  
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Thessaly, Volos, Greece  
A R T I C L E I N F O  
A B S T R A C T  
Keywords:  
Timing analysis is an essential verification method for the design and optimization of integrated circuits,  
while it also constitutes the cornerstone of the final signoff. However, the increasing complexity of modern  
designs, driven by aggressive technology scaling, necessitates more efficient timing engines. In this article,  
we introduce TimeBoost, a novel learning-based approach for performance optimization of gate-level timing  
analysis. TimeBoost employs GPU-accelerated XGBoost classifiers to evaluate stage complexity and select the  
optimal timing analysis method, exploiting the available runtime and accuracy trade-off. Unlike prior works  
that predict timing directly, our approach ensures reliable and accurate timing estimation by leveraging timing  
analysis methods as prediction targets. Moreover, our models utilize representative electrical, topological,  
and gate features for each stage, which can be extracted with minimal effort. We assess several Machine  
Learning (ML) models, including XGBoost, Artificial Neural Networks, and K-Nearest Neighbors, and explore  
their different hyperparameters. Experimental evaluation on one million stages demonstrates that our ML-  
based approach achieves a runtime improvement of up to 3.86× over our best available method, imposing  
only a 3%–4% overhead to timing analysis while maintaining high accuracy. Thus, the proposed models can  
be integrated into signoff tools or timing-driven iterative optimization flows to expedite timing closure.  
Static timing analysis  
Timing optimization  
Gate models  
Effective capacitance  
Interconnect  
Machine learning  
Classification  
XGBoost  
Graphics processing unit  
1. Introduction  
Industrial gate models, such as the Non-Linear Delay Model (NLDM)  
and the more detailed Composite Current Source (CCS) model [7],  
Timing analysis is a critical and demanding process in the design  
and verification of Very Large Scale Integrated (VLSI) circuits, which  
ensures that design timing constraints (e.g., setup and hold) are met.  
It plays a key role in iterative optimization flows, where a timing  
engine is repeatedly invoked at each step to verify and maintain timing  
integrity, while it also serves as the cornerstone of the final signoff  
that determines whether a chip is ready for fabrication [1]. As VLSI  
designs grow in complexity, incorporating billions of transistors and  
employing hundreds of analysis scenarios, timing analysis has emerged  
as the major bottleneck in achieving design closure, accounting for  
up to 60% of the total design time [2]. Moreover, as technologies  
scale below 7 nm, increased wire resistivity, signal non-linearities, the  
Miller effect, and strong gate-interconnect interdependencies render  
accurate timing analysis even more challenging [3–5]. Therefore, the  
efficiency and accuracy of a timing engine are crucial to achieving  
timing closure within reasonable turnaround times. In practice, both  
static [1] and dynamic [6] timing analysis is typically performed at the  
stage level, where each stage is composed of a driver gate, a distributed  
RC interconnect, and a single or multiple receiver gates. Stage timing  
analysis is further divided into two distinct but interdependent parts,  
namely gate and interconnect analysis.  
are utilized to compute the driver output waveform and estimate  
gate timing. However, these models require the approximation of the  
distributed RC interconnect as a lumped capacitive load. The sim-  
plest load model is the sum of all interconnect capacitance values  
(ꢀtotal), while more accurate approaches involve the calculation of an  
effective capacitance (ꢀeff) [3,4,8,9] at the cost of additional runtime  
and memory overhead. On the other hand, the golden standard for  
interconnect analysis is SPICE simulation [10,11], but it is prohibitive  
due to its computational demands. To address this challenge, Model-  
Order Reduction (MOR) techniques [12–14] are commonly employed  
to create a Reduced-Order Model (ROM) of the interconnect, enabling  
more efficient simulation compared to using the original RC circuit.  
The most well-known methods, AWE and RICE [13,14], match the  
first few moments of the interconnect transfer function and create  
a ROM to approximate its transient behavior. Although both tech-  
niques can be highly accurate, they require significant computations  
and are prone to numerical instability, especially when using higher-  
order approximations. Instead, faster analytical moment-based [15–18]  
or ꢀeff-based [4,9] interconnect delay and slew metrics are typically

(G. Stamoulis).

A. Vagenas et al.  
AEUE - International Journal of Electronics and Communications 200 (2025) 155870  
utilized. Therefore, selecting a suitable combination of gate and load  
model, as well as interconnect analysis method, is crucial for balancing  
the accuracy and runtime of gate-level timing analysis.  
proposed models can be integrated into signoff tools or timing-driven  
iterative optimization flows to expedite timing closure.  
The rest of this article is organized as follows. Section 2 provides the  
necessary background material on gate and interconnect timing analy-  
sis. In Section 3, we introduce our ML-based approach for performance  
optimization of timing analysis and evaluate different models and  
their respective hyperparameters. Section 4 presents our experimental  
results, while Section 5 concludes this work.  
Recent efforts to accelerate timing analysis harness parallel comput-  
ing, such as CPU multi-threading, GPUs, and CPU–GPU heterogeneous  
computing, exploiting the available parallelism within timing analysis  
tasks [19]. For instance, enhancing the efficiency of Multi-Corner Multi-  
Mode (MMCM) analysis [20], Path-Based Analysis (PBA) [21], or delay  
calculation [20–22] can lead to speedups of up to three orders of  
magnitude. However, such approaches are closely tied to the underly-  
ing timing analysis method, requiring significant modifications to the  
engine’s data structures and algorithms. Another mainstream approach  
for improving accuracy and efficiency is Machine Learning (ML) [23,  
24], which has been extensively employed in characterization [25,26],  
modeling of complex effects [27,28], and timing estimation [4,29–32].  
More specifically, the availability of scalable frameworks [33–35] and  
the ability of learning-based models to represent multi-dimensional  
data and complex relationships is particularly valuable for intercon-  
nect [29–31] and stage [4,32] timing estimation. The initial approach  
in [29] attempts to reduce the number of expensive signoff tool invoca-  
tions by utilizing a linear regression model with several moment-based  
metrics as inputs. The authors in [30] used Random Forest regressors  
to estimate pre-routing timing and explored different features. In [31],  
Graph-Neural Networks (GNN) were used to accurately predict inter-  
connect delay during signoff, considering interconnect topology and  
RC characteristics. Nevertheless, these works [29–31] focus only on  
interconnect timing, assuming accurate gate timing, and require the ex-  
traction and computation of multiple features before inference, such as  
moment-based metrics [29], driver and receiver locations [30], or inter-  
connect topology and RC values [31]. To sidestep these limitations, [4,  
32] focus on predicting stage timing, considering gate-interconnect  
interdependencies through their input features. The winners of the ACM  
TAU 2021 contest [4,36] leverage an efficient algorithm to extract stage  
features and provide initial stage timing estimates, and then employ  
ANNs to correlate with golden SPICE results. Similarly, [32] proposes  
a custom ANN model, which integrates Transformers and Residual  
Networks (ResNet), to estimate stage delay using multiple moment-  
based and ꢀeff-based features. A critical issue of most ML-based timing  
analysis methods is that they make direct predictions [4,29–32], which  
might be unreliable when the features deviate from the model’s train-  
ing data space. Furthermore, they require large and diverse datasets  
that are often difficult to obtain, while the training process may de-  
mand considerable computational resources. Thus, although ML is  
a powerful tool for timing prediction, ensuring reliability is a per-  
sistent challenge. Finally, the authors in [37] utilized two filtering  
techniques to choose among different gate and interconnect algorithms.  
However, these techniques are applied independently, disregarding  
gate-interconnect interdependencies, while they assume pi-model loads  
and rely on heuristic moment-based thresholds.  
Fig. 1. A gate-level stage with multiple fanout receivers.  
2. Background  
Fig. 1 illustrates a gate-level stage composed of a driver gate, a  
distributed RC interconnect, and multiple receiver gates. Each node ꢁ of  
the RC-tree interconnect has a capacitance to ground ꢀꢁ, a downstream  
capacitance ꢀ  
(i.e., the sum of all the capacitances of the subtree  
tot  
starting at nodeꢁ ꢁ), a slew value ꢂꢁ, and is connected to each fanout  
node ꢃ via a resistance ꢄꢁ→ꢃ. The sum of all interconnect ꢄꢁ→ꢃ values  
is denoted as ꢄ  
.
total  
During stage timing estimation, gate analysis calculates the driver’s  
output voltage waveform to determine the driver delay and slew,  
while interconnect analysis estimates the target receiver’s slew and  
interconnect delay. Together, these values yield the overall stage delay  
(i.e., the sum of the driver and interconnect delay) and slew (i.e., target  
receiver slew). Then, the computed stage delay is utilized to estimate  
the overall delay across various timing paths in the circuit, while the  
stage slew is used as input to the next gate-level stage [1].  
2.1. Gate timing  
Library-compatible gate models, such as NLDM and CCS [3], capture  
the timing and input capacitance information of each gate into Look-Up  
Tables (LUTs) indexed by input slew and output load. These pre-  
characterized LUTs are used by gate-level timing analysis engines for  
accurate stage delay and slew estimation. A brief overview of the  
industrial NLDM and CCS models is provided below.  
• NLDM driver model captures the driver output slew/delay as  
a function of driver input slew (ꢂ ) and output load (ꢀdoruvt).  
in  
drv  
In this article, we introduce TimeBoost, a novel ML-based approach  
to enhance the efficiency of gate-level timing analysis, which is an ex-  
tended version of our previous work [38]. We employ GPU-accelerated  
XGBoost classifiers to analyze stage complexity and select the opti-  
mal timing analysis method. By opting for simpler methods in low-  
complexity stages, we can improve the efficiency of timing analysis  
without compromising accuracy. Unlike related works that predict  
timing directly, TimeBoost ensures reliable and accurate estimation.  
Moreover, it utilizes representative electrical and topological stage  
features, avoiding unnecessary computations such as ꢀeff. To achieve  
this, we assess several ML models, including XGBoost (XGB), Artificial  
Neural Networks (ANN), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and explore  
their different hyperparameters. Experimental results indicate that our  
XGBoost models achieve a runtime improvement of up to 3.86× over  
our best available method, introducing an overall overhead of only  
3%–4% to timing analysis while maintaining high accuracy. Thus, the  
Only a single value is used for slew/delay modeling; hence, the  
non-linearities of the driver output waveform cannot be effec-  
tively modeled. As shown in Fig. 2, this information is stored  
in cell_rise/fall and rise/fall_transition LUTs for  
gate delay and slew, respectively.  
• NLDM receiver model uses a single input pin capacitance (ꢀꢅ)  
value to model the non-linear receiver transistor input capaci-  
tance. As depicted in Fig. 2, the NLDM receiver capacitance value  
is stored in the rise/fall_capacitance fields of each stan-  
dard cell. Unfortunately, this model cannot accurately capture the

2.1.1. Modeling the RC interconnect load  
During standard cell characterization, a lumped capacitive load is  
typically assumed [39,40]. Thus, a single capacitance value must be  
used to represent the highly resistive RC interconnect of each stage  
and access the LUTs. The simplest and most straightforward approxi-  
mation of an RC interconnect load is ꢀtotal, which ignores the resistive  
shielding effect of complex on-chip interconnects, resulting in increased  
pessimism during gate timing analysis [3].  
2.2. Interconnect timing  
SPICE simulation [10,11] is widely regarded as the golden standard  
for interconnect timing estimation, offering the most accurate and  
reliable results. However, its computational requirements, particularly  
in terms of runtime and memory, render it impractical for interconnect  
timing analysis. In contrast, several analytical moment-based models,  
such as the Elmore delay and slew, D2M, and S2M, have been proposed  
for fast timing analysis of interconnects [15,16]. These are based on  
the first few moments of the RC interconnect impulse response, which,  
however, assume step inputs and ignore the resistive shielding effect.  
For each node ꢁ of the ꢄꢀ-tree, the ꢃth (ꢍ(ꢃꢁ)) moment can be recursively  
calculated by:  
To overcome the limitations of ꢀtotal, the concept of ꢀ is often  
eff  
utilized [3,4,8,9], which is an equivalent capacitance that can be  
used as a load to approximate gate timing. In the original approach  
introduced in [8], the RC interconnect is first approximated by a ꢇ-  
model, and then ꢀ is computed by equating the charge absorbed by  
eff  
eff  
the ꢇ-model and ꢀ up to the delay threshold (i.e., 0.5ꢆdd). However,  
a single ꢀ value focuses primarily on delay estimation and thus  
cannot acceuffrately approximate slew, which is vital for interconnect  
analysis [3], as it assumes that the driver output voltage waveform  
is a linear ramp. Moreover, it ignores the Miller effect and neglects  
the impact of receiver input capacitance ꢀꢅ on ꢀeff, delay, and slew  
estimation. To this end, the authors in [3] introduced a CCS-based

Furthermore, the approach of [4] extends the slew metric of [9] for  
multiple voltage regions. For each node ꢁ of the RC-tree, the slew of  
complexity of each gate-level stage based on representative stage fea-  
tures. The ML model identifies the most suitable method that effectively  
exploits the available accuracy and runtime trade-offs, which is then  
applied by the timing analysis engine during stage delay and slew  
estimation. Stages with lower complexity can be analyzed using simple  
yet fast methods, such as ꢀtotal, moment-based metrics, and NLDM  
driver and receiver models. In contrast, higher-complexity stages can  
utilize more accurate but computationally expensive methodologies,  
such as multiple ꢀ and slew values, along with CCS driver and re-  
ceiver models. As aerfef sult, gate-level timing analysis can be significantly  
accelerated without accuracy loss. More specifically, our ML models  
determine the least computationally expensive method that meets a  
target accuracy threshold ꢏ (e.g., ꢏ < 2%). This threshold is based  
on the mean of stage delay and slew Relative Error (RE) against a  
golden reference (e.g., SPICE or signoff STA tool) and guides the classi-  
fier’s decision-making process. If no method meets this requirement,  
the model defaults to the most accurate available method. Our ML  
classifiers use five stage timing analysis methods (M1-M5) as target  
output classes, offering different levels of accuracy and efficiency. As  
listed in Table 1, the employed methods utilize different gate and  
interconnect timing analysis techniques, each implemented using the  
iterative procedure described in Section 2.3. Note that we used Eq. (1)  
for ꢀ computation (in single or multiple regions), Eqs. (2) and (3)  
for alelffmoment-based metrics, Eq. (4) for the ꢀeff-Elmore metric, and  
Eq. (5) for the slew values of M1 and M2 (in single or multiple regions).  
Finally, M1 was configured using three voltage regions, i.e., [0, ꢆ ],

Utilizing the above formula, the non-linear voltage waveforms can be  
approximated using multiple slew values, which enables more accurate  
interconnect timing analysis compared to moment-based metrics [15,  
16].  
2.3. Stage timing analysis  
Considering the previous discussion, a stage timing analysis method-  
ology has to combine the appropriate gate timing model (e.g., CCS,  
NLDM), load approximation method (e.g., ꢀtotal, ꢀeff), and interconnect  
analysis algorithm to effectively estimate stage delay and slew. The  
strong gate-interconnect interdependencies, which are evident in Eqs.  
(1) and (5), necessitate an iterative approach that can incrementally  
refine the ꢀeff, slew, and delay values on each node of the RC inter-  
connect. Consequently, [4,9] propose an iterative refinement procedure  
that effectively addresses these issues and accurately estimates stage  
timing. Fig. 3 outlines the main steps involved in this process:  
1. Driver output waveform estimation: Given the driver out-  
put ꢀ  
(initially ꢀtotal) load approximation, NLDM or CCS  
driveremff odel LUTs are indexed to compute the output waveform  
(i.e., driver delay and slew value(s)).

form is propagated through the RC-tree to each receiver in-  
put, utilizing interconnect timing metrics (e.g., Eqs. (3)–(5)) to  
determine the delay and slew values on each node.

3.1. Input features  
rcv  
model LUTs and compute the input pin capacitance values (ꢀꢅ)  
for all the receivers.  
During ML model development, the selection of input features is  
typically more crucial than the ML algorithm itself. The most critical  
features exhibit strong correlation with the target output, enabling the  
generation of accurate and high-performance models that require fewer  
data points and less training time. Table 2 lists the input features of our  
proposed classifiers, which focus on capturing the gate, electrical, and  
topological characteristics of the <driver, RC interconnect, receiver(s)>  
stage. Firstly, in order to provide information about the driver and its  
output voltage waveform, we incorporate the cell type (e.g., NAND2),  
driver input slew, and type of transition at the output. During our  
experimentation, additional features like the drive strength did not  
boost prediction accuracy, while incorporating CCS or NLDM LUT  
information considerably increased inference time and model size. Sec-  
ondly, we extract key attributes of the RC interconnect, which include  
minimum, maximum, mean, and variance of the ꢀꢁ and ꢄꢁ→ꢃ values,  
4. Backward ꢀꢁꢂꢂ propagation: Given the ꢀꢅ and slew values, ꢀ  
eff  
values on each node are updated using Eq. (1).  
This procedure is repeated until the driver output ꢀ or slew  
eff  
value(s) converge within a specified threshold. Note that this approach  
is highly flexible, accommodating different combinations of gate and  
interconnect algorithms. For example, [9] employs a single ꢀ and  
slew value along with NLDM, while [4] utilizes 3 ꢀ and slewevffalues  
eff  
with CCS to improve accuracy. Alternatively, a more computationally  
efficient approach might utilize ꢀ

achieve a different level of accuracy and efficiency depending on the  
complexity of the examined stage.

In this section, we present TimeBoost, our ML-driven methodology  
for optimizing the performance of timing analysis. As shown in Fig.  
3, the proposed approach employs XGBoost classifiers to evaluate the

1
paths, the selected number of values offers a good balance between  
accuracy and efficiency in terms of ML model performance. Note that  
for paths consisting of less than ten segments, the remaining path-based  
values are set to zero to minimize their impact on selecting the optimal  
method. Moreover, we incorporate the D2M delay value of the target  
receiver as well as the total number of receivers in the stage (fanout).  
The extraction of the above features incurs minimal overhead, as most  
can be directly obtained from the design files (i.e., Verilog, SPEF,  
Liberty) or computed with a small amount of work. For example, driver-  
related features are, by definition, inputs to the stage timing analysis  
engine (e.g., driver input slew, driver cell type), while interconnect or  
path-based features can be easily computed during the construction and  
initialization of the RC interconnect graph.  
estimators (ꢐ), ANNs employed three distinct architectures, while  
KNNs utilized three different algorithms. The ANNs were trained using  
the Adam optimizer [41], a maximum number of 2000 epochs, and  
the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function for hidden layers  
and softmax for the output. Note that in this article, the reported  
architecture refers to the hidden layers used, as the input and output  
layers depend on the number of input features and output classes,  
respectively. For example, an ANN with a [128, 128] architecture  
has an input layer with 45 inputs, two dense hidden layers with 128  
neurons, and an output layer with five outputs. The rest of the model  
hyperparameters for the ANN and XGB models (e.g., batch size for  
ANNs and leaf samples for XGB) were selected via Bayesian search  
and optimization. Our dataset consisted of one million stages, with  
90% allocated for training and 10% for testing. For each stage in  
the dataset, we identified the method with the shortest runtime that  
met the specified threshold ꢏ or, if not found, we selected the most  
accurate one among all five methods. Before training, we normalized  
all input features using the standard scaler [33] to ensure that no input  
dominates over the others. Finally, model generation was performed  
using the XGBoost [35], PyTorch [34], and Sklearn [33] frameworks,  
3.2. Model evaluation  
To assess the effectiveness of our feature selection and modeling,  
we investigated three types of ML models, namely, XGBoost (XGB),  
Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)  
classifiers. For each model type, we varied different hyperparameters  
and accuracy thresholds ꢏ (2%–8% RE against SPICE), generating mul-  
tiple models. Specifically, the XGB models were trained using 40–180  
5
   
A. Vagenas et al.  
AEUE - International Journal of Electronics and Communications 200 (2025) 155870  
Fig. 4. Accuracy (F1 score), inference and training times, and memory requirements of all the evaluated ML models.  
utilizing CPU acceleration for KNN and GPU acceleration for XGB/ANN  
training and inference.1  
increasing the number of estimators in XGB models or the number  
of neurons in ANNs leads to longer inference and training times, as  
well as greater disk space requirements for model storage. In the case  
of KNNs, no actual weights are stored during training. Instead, the  
entire dataset is memorized, and optionally, an index is generated  
to access the data points more efficiently, depending on the selected  
algorithm. During inference, for each input to the model, the algorithm  
determines the output class based on the k-nearest neighbors. Thus,  
KNNs are less suitable for optimizing gate-level timing analysis, as they  
exhibit significantly higher inference time compared to XGB and ANNs.  
Additionally, model size is highly dependent on the number of features  
and data points, which scales with the size of the dataset. Conversely,  
the ANN and XGB models are high-performance, have a low memory  
footprint, and can utilize both CPU and GPU acceleration. XGB models,  
in particular, require minimal hyperparameter tuning, can be trained  
easily in resource-constrained environments, and offer superior accu-  
racy compared to ANNs. Considering these observations, we selected  
XGB models with 120 estimators for each ML model configuration, as  
they provide the best balance between accuracy (high F1 score) and  
performance (low inference/training time).  
Figs. 4(a)–4(c) illustrate the accuracy (F1 score), training and in-  
ference times, and memory requirements of the evaluated ML models.  
As can be seen, the XGB models outperform ANNs and KNNs, with the  
more complex (having 120 or more estimators) and those employing  
higher threshold criteria exhibiting the highest scores (F1 up to 0.934).  
Increasing the number of estimators enhances predictive performance  
while targeting a higher accuracy threshold favors selecting the optimal  
timing analysis method. This highlights the robustness of XGB mod-  
els, particularly in modeling highly variable, non-continuous spaces  
using tabular input data. Moreover, Figs. 5(a)–5(b) further demonstrate  
the impact of our features, showcasing the substantial feature gain  
of our selection and the excellent classification performance of the  
XGB models. In comparison, KNNs and ANNs exhibit similar accuracy  
trends, with accuracy only improving as the threshold ꢏ increases,  
achieving F1 up to 0.853 and 0.881, respectively. As shown in Figs.  
4(b)–4(c), the deterministic nature of the evaluated KNN algorithms  
ensures consistent prediction outcomes, whereas increasing the number  
of neurons in the ANN hidden layers leads to minor accuracy variations  
(e.g., [128, 128] vs. [256, 256]). Notably, more complex ANN config-  
urations sometimes performed worse at higher thresholds (i.e., ꢏ <  
8%) due to overfitting issues and the significant effort required for  
hyperparameter tuning.  
4. Experimental evaluation  
To evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed ML-driven approach,  
we compared it against the base methods listed in Table 1, which  
also serve as the target output classes. For the ML approach, we  
implemented two models, ML1 and ML2, targeting 2% and 4% accuracy  
threshold ꢏ, respectively. All base methods, along with the ML mod-  
els, were integrated into the C++ framework of the ACM TAU 2021  
contest [42], which generates representative stages of VLSI circuits  
and facilitates the evaluation of stage timing analysis methodologies.  
Inference/training times and model size are influenced primarily  
by the complexity of the underlying model (XGB and ANN) or algo-  
rithm (KNN) rather than the accuracy threshold criteria. For instance,  
1
More details about the dataset and the experimental setup can be found  
in Section 4.  
6
       
A. Vagenas et al.  
AEUE - International Journal of Electronics and Communications 200 (2025) 155870  
Fig. 5. Feature gain and confusion matrices of XGB models under different thresholds ꢏ.  
The framework allows for parallel computation of stage timing using  
CPU acceleration through multi-threading [43], and the ML models  
can leverage either CPU or GPU acceleration during inference by set-  
ting a single configuration parameter [35]. Specifically, the ML-based  
methods were evaluated under both acceleration scenarios to assess  
their performance, while M1-M5 exploit only CPU acceleration. Our  
experiments were conducted on one million representative stages that  
cover various driver/receiver combinations and interconnect topolo-  
gies. The driver and receiver gates are selected from the ASU ASAP  
7 nm, Silvaco FreePDK 15 nm, and Silvaco FreePDK 45 nm Process  
Design Kits (PDKs) [44,45]. The interconnects vary in length and are  
routed across multiple metal layers, with up to 16 layers available  
depending on the PDK. Consequently, the RC values of the interconnect  
models span a wide range, determined by the resistance and capaci-  
tance of the selected metal layers and the resistance of the connecting  
vias. Moreover, the stages are generated so that driver input slew  
Considering the runtime results, we can observe a clear correlation  
between the complexity of each base method and its respective perfor-  
mance. In particular, M1 exhibits the highest runtime (12.5 s), whereas  
the simplest M3 method records the lowest (0.55 s). Moreover, our  
proposed ML approaches, ML1 and ML2, achieve substantial speedups  
compared to the most accurate method (M1) while maintaining high ac-  
curacy levels. With CPU-accelerated inference, ML1 and ML2 expedite  
timing analysis by 2.29× and 3.28×, respectively. When leveraging GPU  
acceleration, the speedups increase to 2.62× and 3.86×, respectively,  
representing a 15% improvement over the CPU-accelerated imple-  
mentation. Furthermore, the overall overhead introduced by the ML  
model to timing analysis is reduced to 3%–4%, compared to 8%–10%  
corresponding to CPU-accelerated execution. Therefore, when GPU  
hardware is available, the resulting performance gains can expedite  
timing analysis without compromising predictive accuracy or ease of  
use. It is crucial to note that adopting more resource-intensive signoff-  
accurate methods, such as AWE and RICE [13,14], as output classes of  
our ML models would further amplify the performance and improve the  
accuracy of the proposed approach. Since computationally expensive  
methods demand longer runtimes, selecting more efficient alternatives  
yields greater relative speedups, as model inference represents a con-  
stant yet smaller cost compared to the total stage timing analysis  
time. Thus, this further enhances the practicality and effectiveness of  
our ML models, particularly in performance-critical applications like  
timing-driven iterative optimization flows [1].  
in  
(ꢂ ), driver output effective capacitance (ꢀeff), and receiver output  
drv  
capacitance (ꢀrocuvt) cover the entire range of the precharacterized LUTs  
and even beyond that. The golden stage delay and slew values were  
obtained using the Xyce Parallel Electronic Simulator [10]. Finally,  
all experiments were run on a Linux workstation, equipped with a  
3.70 GHz 12-thread Ryzen 5600X CPU, an NVIDIA RTX 3700 GPU, and  
16 GB of RAM.  
Table 3 presents the mean stage delay and slew RE against SPICE,  
the runtime, and the speedup over the most accurate method (M1)  
achieved by each approach. Firstly, it is evident that accuracy decreases  
as we transition from the most complex multiple ꢀeff-based (M1) to the  
simplest ꢀtotal-based (M3) method. Secondly, moment-based methods  
(M3-M5) generally exhibit lower accuracy, with M4-M5 being rarely  
selected by the ML models, as depicted in the confusion matrices of Fig.  
5. In contrast, our ML approaches achieve accuracy in line with their  
respective thresholds and our available methods. ML1 closely follows  
the best available method (M1), exhibiting stage delay and slew RE of  
2.51% and 2.85%, respectively. Moreover, ML2 effectively maintains  
errors well below the target 4% threshold, achieving 3.39% and 3.87%  
stage delay and slew RE. Note that ML1 and ML2 achieve the same  
accuracy when utilizing CPU or GPU acceleration, as it does not affect  
accuracy, but only runtime performance.  
5. Conclusions  
In this work, we presented TimeBoost, a simple yet powerful  
learning-based approach to accelerate timing analysis. The proposed  
method employs XGBoost classifiers, which, given representative fea-  
tures of a gate-level stage, predict the most suitable timing analysis  
method, exploiting the available accuracy/runtime trade-offs. Exper-  
imental results demonstrate that our ML-driven approach delivers a  
3.86× speedup over our best available stage timing analysis method  
while maintaining commendable accuracy.

Nestor Evmorfopoulos is an Associate Professor at the  
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering of the  
University of Thessaly, Greece. He received the B.E., M.Sc.,  
and Ph.D. degrees in Electrical and Computer Engineering  
from the National Technical University of Athens. His main  
research interests are focused on mathematical and compu-  
tational methods for CAD tools and algorithms for integrated  
circuit design, with emphasis on large-scale circuit, thermal,  
electromagnetic and physical simulation. He has published  
over 100 articles in international journals and conferences

Project/asap7/. [Accessed 16 April 2025].  
[45] Silvaco - Open-Cell 15 nm and 45 nm FreePDK Libraries, [Online]. Available:  
https://si2.org/open-cell-library/. [Accessed 12 June 2025].  
Georgios (George) Stamoulis received his Diploma from  
the National Technical University of Athens in 1989. He was  
awarded the M.S. (1991) and Ph.D. (1994) degrees from the  
University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. After one year  
as a Visiting Assistant Professor at the University of Iowa, he  
joined Intel Corp. In 2001 he was elected Assistant Professor  
at the Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering  
at the Technical University of Crete. In 2003 he became  
an Associate Professor at the Department of Computer  
and Telecommunications Engineering at the University of  
Thessaly. In 2009 he became Professor. From 2013 to 2018  
he was the Head of the newly formed Computer Science  
Department and from 2018 to June 2023 the Dean of the  
School of Engineering of the University of Thessaly. Since  
2023 he is a Member of the Management Council of the  
University of Thessaly. His research interests focus on the  
analysis and optimization of average and maximum power  
of integrated circuits, the analysis and optimization of the  
maximum voltage drop on the power supply lines of inte-  
grated circuits, low power design, reliability analysis and  
optimization, and the application of massively parallel and  
deep-learning techniques to the aforementioned problems.  
He has founded two startups, authored over 250 papers in  
journals and major conferences, claims three US patents and  
has over 2500 citations to his work.  
Anastasis Vagenas received his M.Eng. from the Depart-  
ment of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University  
of Thessaly, Volos, Greece, in 2022. He is currently  
a
Ph.D. candidate in the same department and focuses on  
Machine Learning techniques for efficient optimization and  
modeling in timing analysis. His research interests include  
timing and power analysis, signal integrity, and standard  
cell pre-characterization.  
Dimitrios Garyfallou is a Postdoctoral Researcher and Ad-  
junct Lecturer at the Department of Electrical and Computer  
Engineering of the University of Thessaly, Greece. He earned  
his Diploma, M.Sc., and Ph.D. from the same department  
in 2014, 2015, and 2021, respectively. His research fo-  
cuses on algorithms and machine learning techniques for  
Electronic Design Automation (EDA), with emphasis on  
timing and power analysis, circuit reliability, and large-scale  
simulation. He was a Research Assistant at the Center for  
Research and Technology - Hellas (CERTH) in 2015 and a  
Visiting Research Associate at Queen’s University Belfast,  
U.K., in 2017 and 2019. Dimitrios has authored over 23  
articles in international journals and conferences, received  
2 best paper award nominations, served on technical and  
organizing committees for international conferences, and  
9
